speaker: interviewer
text: --September, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia. And the camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we were talking about the day that your father was taken away. You said that he was two houses away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: From your home. Did you actually see it happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't see it happening. Neighbors came and told us that-- my father was very well-known in the street. And then, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just opposite us was the horse exercising building of the academy. And our house had two little openings on the roof. And I was able to go up there and look through, and I seen my father there. The evening, a military policeman banged on the door and came in about half-drunk with a note with-- from my father, asking us to give that man his equipment. Naturally, at that time, all Hungarian men-- Jew or no Jew-- had the equipment ready that, if they have to go to the Army or battalion, they have. So there was my father backpack with conserves, and covers, and this and that. And we gave that man-- naturally, the note also said to give him money. And I know, I seen it that my father got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- were you able to give him a note, or anything, with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, I don't remember. I only remember that he was there still early morning. And then he went and took the backpack. And my father received it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your father know that you were there and could see him? Did he look out the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so. I don't think so. And the next thing what we knew about my father, we got a card from [? Walze. ?] What-- after the war, we realized that it's Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [? Walze ?] was a code name for Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Walze ?] was the code name that that card came from, saying that he is OK. And it was his writing, because he has a very special way of-- of writing. And we recognized it. And we also recognized a number of other family members' signatures on the card, with whom he was probably meet up there. And that's about what we know about my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to receive that card, was that any kind of a comfort to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that stage, we thought that it's OK. It's a work camp. And as my father was not a fat-- somebody who sat always in an office, but a well-built somebody. And we thought that, because he has the experience of POW camp in First World War in Siberia, we had very high hopes that, in a work camp situation, he was be able to stand up and survive it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how soon after he'd been taken away did you get that card?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got that card somewhere in July, early July. I don't-- I don't remember exact dates.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he taken? What month?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was taken in early July, if-- if my recollection right. And that letter came somewhere on the 20-- 25th of-- of July. Somehow, that is the number what stuck in my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So would you say that it was after that time that life became more difficult for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because Dad was able to organize better and look after better of the whole family group. Grandpa was already a more elderly gentleman, who walked with a stick and had very thick glasses. And Father was the one who was able to-- to look more after the family and organize things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you miss him very much?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Still, until now. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you say that you had to grow up very quickly after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I think so, because my mother was absolutely devastated. And the family tried to build me up, that now you are the man of the house, and you have to look after, and this and that. So naturally, a 12-year-old kid can't do much looking after. But at least, I think, they got with it that I kept the kids OK. And that's how we went on reasonably still-- still OK. Then came October and the change from the Horthy regime to the Szalasi regime. And the Arrow Cross took over the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we talk about that, can I just ask you-- at this time after the German occupation, had you kept up the Jewish rituals and the Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, we still had the kosher home. Maybe with less meat, and but strictly kosher only. At the-- more at the end of it, by-- by October, that food became a bit scarce. And the-- and the parents became worried that we children, growing children, don't get enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after we had dinner and grandpa said his prayers, the kids were taken out to a little side room where we had special wooden plates and special little knives. And we were given some szalonna, what is pork fat, smoked, what is to give you energy and grow. And that was strictly only eaten all there with-- because that was [? terrible. ?] But that was only for the three of us children. And was the fact that you did keep this up and-- and still had Shabbat, was that helpful to you and-- and provided some kind of strength for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I think so, because we tried to stick as much to the normal as it is-- as it was possible. And we-- we did it at home, because to go to the synagogue, there was-- I don't think there was even synagogue by then. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that difficult to do? Were you in any danger by lighting the candles and that sort of thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, inside our house-- look. It was-- it was air raid time, so the windows was very blackened anyway. And-- and you can't look-- you couldn't look into our-- our house, because even in normal times, wooden covers were on our-- particularly on our windows, to close off the outside at the evening. And-- and the house was closed. We had a closed house. And only we were in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you were saying that the Arrow Cross took over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did life change for you after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. It wasn't much change. It was even more restricted to go out. It was even more difficult to get food, but we still got food. And-- and the only thing started that they started to take the women into work battalions. And they did it in the easiest way. You have to report in this and that sports ground. And they started to organize and take them away from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: From our house, four of the women was in the age group. What was? My mother, my mother's two sister, and Roszi, who was my auntie, mom's brother's wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now somehow, when it came to the sports ground, they realized that they can't handle such big crowd, or this or that. But they started to say that whose husband is in work battalion can go home. So Roszi and [? Bergi, ?] their husbands were in work battalion. They said they go home. And they told my mother that, you should say too your husband is in work camp too. But my mother said, no. It's only on the straight and narrow. And she stayed, and also her younger sister. They go into different groups. Magda was taken down close to Budapest, to start digging tank traps, because the Russians were already getting closer. Mom was put into a work company and marched out. We got a card from her in November. 'I'm all right. I'm going to [NON-ENGLISH].' [TEARFUL] That is from the border. And she knew that she'd be taken to Germany. Magda was working-- digging, whatever-- in [NON-ENGLISH]. And then they started to take them to Germany. But a family hid her and, two days later, brought her back to Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to see her again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Incredible. Incredible. And this family not only brought her home, but brought us food also. I have to say that that part of-- of outer Budapest was basically Germans. We called them Swabs. They were German-Hungarians. And they were, naturally, very German by that time. For some reason, that family was nice and hid Magda, brought up Magda. And the aftermath was that, in 1945, '46, after the war, all these German-Hungarians were sent back to Germany. That family [TEARFUL] was protected, and they stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you helped them to stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Magda helped them to stay and gave it back to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Magda just showed up at the house one day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Magda just showed up at the house with this couple and [BLOWS NOSE] and food for us. So the only one who was missing from grandma's three daughters was my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like for you to have both your parents gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very, very hard to think of them, not knowing what happening. We always lived very close together. And even so, that the rest of the family was there, and they-- you know, we were always very, very close with Magda and [? Bergi. ?] So [? Bergi ?] became my second mother. She still is. [EMOTIONAL] And with Magda, also, we have very, very close ties. Because at that time, young ladies did not go out by themselves into the mountains, or bicycle touring, or the Danube with the little boats. So she needed a gardedam. And I was the one.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from very young age, I was spending a lot of time with her, touring, bicycle touring, going on the Danube, and all that, with the boyfriends. So she-- because of that, she had much freer movement, and I had a hell of a lot of fun. And that kept us always very close. So you had people there for support?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you and your sister support for each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were you still in the same house at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were still in the same house. We were in the same house till December. December, the Hungarian Arrow Cross started to set up the ghetto. Now-- [WIPING EYES]

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the yellow-star house, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were living in the yellow-star house in [NON-ENGLISH] next to the academy. And in December, they started to put up the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's December 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's 1944. And at the same time, there were a lot of shutzpasses. Different embassies issued a letter of protection to people. We had-- all of us had letters of protection from the [NON-ENGLISH]-- because Arpad, my mother's brother, was in a work battalion at Budapest. And the whole battalion was under the [NON-ENGLISH] protection.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that entitled your entire family to the same protection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through that-- I think it was a scheme. And I think the boys in the battalion, they made up these papers. But it was reasonably accepted, like Wallenberg's papers. There were real papers, there was papers that was just made up. It was the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were fake papers, you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically, I think, yes. So when this ghetto thing came on, that we have to move into the ghetto, the same time, it came out that those people who are protected can go into a protected area. What was basically a [NON-ENGLISH]. And that part of Budapest were-- were big, flat houses. And then, suddenly, they opened up Swedish, and Swiss, and-- and different protected houses. Also the [NON-ENGLISH] and also the Red Cross-- by that time, the Red Cross gave out some sort of shutzpass also. Magda and [? Bergi ?] went in to start to look for place. Time was very limited, because you could be out only between this and that hour. And they went, and they looked. And for some reason, they couldn't get a place in the [NON-ENGLISH] house, but they met up a gentleman, who was the head of the Red Cross-- George [? Otzale, ?] who was a relation. And he very quickly fixed up that we got a room in one of the Red Cross protected houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was he related to you? He's an Appel. Originally, his name was Appel. And he is first or second cousin to Mom. But he was working in the underground. And he was the sort of head of the Red Cross with a Gestapo car and German protective service, and I don't know what. He was helping a lot of Jews out of the [INAUDIBLE]. He was doing a job maybe not at that size as Wallenberg, but something like that. How was he able to do that, if he was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wasn't working under his own name. And there were many Jews in the underground and taking up different names and et cetera. And they were able to do it. There were whole groups of Jewish people who were working for the underground and helping Jews to-- to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can I just ask you-- you said that you thought that the passes were fake. What gave you that idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. But what I learned since then-- how these papers were done, et cetera-- and I know that the work battalion had the facilities to produce that sort of things. And it came through too fast to too many people, so I presumed that they had an original, and they made copies of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you moved into this house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we moved in to [NON-ENGLISH]. I have to tell you the story there, that whatever we could-- we had only one room for the six, eight-- eight of us. So there were no possibility to get somebody to take all things, so they had a hand trolley. And [? Bergi ?] and Roszi was, with Magda was taking that trolley whatever we could-- some beddings, and some food what we still had, to the house. And we three children and the grandparents, we were going by the tram. At that time, the [NON-ENGLISH], the [NON-ENGLISH] Bridge was partly blown. So the end of the tram line was just where the bridge started. And that is where [NON-ENGLISH] is. On the corner of [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] corner, there was a big Arrow Cross building. You couldn't avoid it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we got off the trains, kids came there and started to shoo us toward the entrance of the Arrow Cross house. And there was a big fellow with a submachine gun. And he said, what you want from them? They are kids and elderly. Get rid of them! And sent us on our merry way. Days later, we knew that hundreds of people were taken into that house-- naturally, children and elderly, because nobody else was moving by that time-- and taken to the Danube and shot. Why he sent us away? [EMOTIONAL] Who knows? And that's how we got into [NON-ENGLISH] 23, what was the Red Cross protected house. How many Red Cross houses were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two.

speaker: interviewee
text: That house is six floor high, very elegant building, with lift, I think, three flats in every floor. And it was packed. It was packed like you've never seen anything like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me some idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. We had a hole. A hole is-- is a very small room. Look. When we went to bed on the floor, then, when we wanted to turn, then we have to say that let's turn. And we all of us turned. And all that was packed up at daytime, into a corner, so there was some room to move. And also, it has the beauty of it that it has a flat roof. So at nighttime, we can lay down on the flat roof with a stopwatch and measure it how far the Russian artillery is-- because they have, by that time, real close. As you may know, by-- by Christmas Eve, Budapest was completely circled by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was this a time of hope, in a way, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. Hope and worry that they could not distinguish between our building and building where there was no Jews. So we spent quite a bit of time in the under part of the building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a cellar there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A cellar-- in the cellar. Cooking-- whatever cooking there was was out in the open. We broke up some furnishings. And they said that, because gas did not exist. I think electricity-- there was still electricity.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you say you did the cooking out in the open, did you actually have to have a fire there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just in the room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the room. No, no, no, no, no. Out-- out in the yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside, in the yard. And twice, the Arrow Cross tried to take the whole house away. My-- I mean, the people from it. And the Red Cross turned up. And there were always ways and means, luckily, to persuade them other ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the ways and means were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Money, money, and more money, and gold, and whatever they could get together. We knew we-- that, from many houses, they took away people. And it was only one street to the Danube. And they were shooting the people into the Danube, indiscriminately.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow, we were just lucky. And twice, they talked them out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like in terms of hygiene in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the women was-- were very, very hardworking to-- to keep the place clean. Water was still working, so-- excuse me-- toilets were still working. And even with the overload, I don't think there was any special problem. There was quite a number of doctors in the house. And I think it was quite well organized. There were some supervisors out of the people who living there. And I think it was quite well done. I know we still were able to wash and use toilet. And even so, that the Red Cross house was, all of us Jews, for some reason, there was a Christmas party. There was-- Christmas party, because the Red Cross has Christmas presents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of presents did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had some chocolates, and some biscuits, and fruit.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that day like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fantastic. Fantastic. I had a-- a little safe where my father carried the papers what were important. And that, he always took with him when we went down to the bunker. And that stayed with me. I became head of the family. I carried that. And I locked the stuff away in that. That was safe. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it very strange to have a good day amongst all--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the trouble that was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was. And on top of it, one of my father's friend's wife came through the-- Budapest. I don't know if the trams were really still working. And she brought some food to us, too. Mrs. [? Zappomel. ?] Where the name comes from? Mrs. [? Zappomel. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was on that day, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was on that day, as well, because at Christmas, she thought she has to do something for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she wasn't Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, she was a very, very religious Christian lady. And it was a sort of-- the husband was a sort of business friends of my father. And they just lived a corner away from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened in the house, in terms of contact between all the different people that were living there? Did people basically stick to their families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look. We children were running around like crazy. Sometimes, we were able to play in the yard. But a lot of time we spent in the bunker, because, by that time, it was quite strong artillery fire and bombing of-- of-- of Budapest. And it was also cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape three of the interview with Ivan Devai. It's the 17th of September, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia.
